sentences.text: Yes.
Yes.
So as I was alluding to the dichotomy that my father would have between the religious or national-- probably, I would call it national upbringing, Jewish National upbringing.
But it's obviously, not having a nation-- at least in the normal sense-- that was always the glue that kept it together, it was religion.
So needless to say, he had to have that element too, and insisted on it.
And so, when I was bar mitzvahed, I had a tutor, despite the fact that I spoke Hebrew.
And I had a tutor for the particular thing that you have to do to learn during the prayer.
And I have-- and my father, by connections, probably-- friends or whatever-- actually insisted it be done in an orthodox temple-- synagogue, very orthodox.
And I read whatever, you know?
And those of you who are familiar, it is called the Haftarah, which you read the whole thing, and the prayers.
I had, obviously, no difficulty in my-- in the language, itself.
So it wasn't the same thing as probably here, where you have to, through the bar mitzvah, you have to also learn the language.
For me, it was the natural language which I could speak.
But I did go through it.
And I had it.
And of course, then I-- and it was a natural and acquired part of my life in the school, because every kid, at that time, my classmate, had bar mitzvah in the seventh grade.
So needless to say, I wasn't going to be deprived of that either, even if my father didn't care to have it.
But he did.
So I had that.
Yes.
After that, my aunt-- because we didn't have an apartment any more-- my aunt actually threw a party.
And all my classmates came.
As usual, it was the requirement.
And I used to go to my classmates.
I mean, that was the natural exchange.
And so that's about all I remember about that.
And the fountain pens, and the watches, and whatever, went with it.
I remember, for example, a briefcase that I got.
And I carried it through all-- into the ghetto, actually.
So it was a nice, leather briefcase, which was very important for a 13-year-old kid, because, in our times-- now, we have gone back now to the ruck sack.
It's now become very popular.
But in those day, only you carried that when you were a kid.
When you became older, you were going to carry a briefcase.
And so at 13, it was-- probably, that was the passage to manhood, more so [LAUGHS] than anything else.
That why I remember the briefcase.
Well, first of all, we obviously were very deeply immersed in the Polish culture, on an intellectual level.
And as I said, my cousin was already quite well known in the theater in Warsaw.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and I-- is there a problem?
And I have seen-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] as far as meeting people, my father worked with Poles.
With people socially, of course, not that much.

I mean, obviously, the Jewish community-- particularly in Lodz, where there was a third of the population was Jewish-- it's its own institution, its own theaters, its own arts, everything had its own on one hand.
On the other hand, Jews were very heavily, deeply involved in the Polish cultural life.
They were contributors.
I was just having an opportunity to read a book-- I'm not taking too much time-- a book that they issued, that I showed you last time, in Israel, 50 And I saw some pictures there.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was trying to make a connection between it-- would you repeat the question?
It was your family's relationship with the non-Jewish--
Oh, yes.
Yes.
And so I saw a-- a poem by Julian Tuwim.
Now, Julian Tuwim was the premiere poet of Poland right before World War II, during.
Of course, he was in the United States.
He survived here.
And he went back to Poland and became really the poet of Poland.
And I read his poem.
And so I don't know-- when you say connection with the-- with the Polish culture, Polish society, I'm not so sure that I understand the difference.
But if you're talking about Polish culture, definitely, not only connected, but probably contributing.
As a matter of fact, in the poem, he makes a reference to Lodz, because it's in memory of Lodz.
And he was born in Lodz, and so he writes about Lodz.
And he says, among other things, that he says, and there, where I started my life as a poet, what I was getting paid by a-- by a publisher, $0.02 per line.
Well, it strikes me very strange, because the publisher was my father, you know?
And I kind of took exception to that, maybe.
I don't think my father-- what my father did tell me, that during World War I, he did invite Tuwin for dinner, because he was hungry.

And then, when he wrote one of his most famous poems-- which I know to this very day by heart-- so if that, you are referring to connection with the world outside, then definitely there was a deep-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm searching the word for it, but-- Now, as far as Polish friends, yeah, I presume there were such.
My father had a German friend who was of German-- Lodz was divided into three types of population, Jewish, Polish, and German.
And they were all just about equal, one third of each.
And there was three quarter of a million, so it's about a quarter million of each.
And so there were a lot of prominent presence of Germans in the population, I recall.
But I don't remember socializing very much, although I would probably, if I could recall, I would say, yes.
My mother probably had some acquaintances.
But Jews-- with such a heavy Jewish population in the city, definitely, the Jewish life was very strong, very alive and vibrant.
And so probably most of the socializing was within the group.
Oh, yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Well, first of all, I was very-- since I was not that much of a-- of a groupie-- is that the right word?
As a child-- is that correct?
A groupie?
Oh, yes.
Yes.
Right.
Right.
That I would be more contemplative and read a lot.
Was very aware of the political situation.
I even thought, at the age of 10, that most grown-ups are stupid and uninformed.
And unfortunately, I have to say that, to this very day, I didn't have much opportunity to change my mind, but-- at least in large numbers.
Individuals present and others, exempt.
But what I was saying is that I was very much alert of what was going on.
As soon as I could read.
And I read-- by the age of 10, 11, by that time, I read normal literature.
Remember that, in that sense, I was very well educated.
I remember, at the age of 12, I saw this performance.
And it impressed me very much, you know?
So I was aware of literature, whether it was foreign or Polish.
And I read voraciously, and so I-- and papers, as well.
They were always on the table.
There was something always available to read.
And the radio was on.
And so I would certainly listen, and read, and be very much aware of it.
So I knew from the very beginning of rise of Nazism.
Coupled to an anti-Semitism of the country where I lived, it certainly made me aware of all of these things.
By '35, '36, we are starting getting physical evidence, expulsion of Polish-born Jews from Germany.
Some of them wound up in my class-- kids from Germany, one or two, who would show up because they were-- '37, '38.
And of course, starting with the Anschluss, with Austria, and everything else, it was always there.
And I was always, with the news, I was interested.
And I wanted-- and I read a lot.

And I even thought my interpretation, at that time, I thought-- and I'm not so sure I want to change my mind, although I should-- is that I think that I had a better understanding of the times than my father or my uncle, who I mentioned.
My uncle, in 1939, with the storm troopers, were marching in the street already, taking over Poland.
My uncle was reading-- the Berlin [GERMAN], and said that the people who produced Goethe and Beethoven couldn't be that bad.
And I thought, for Pete's sake, where was he for the last eight years?
I mean, doesn't he ever read anything?
Doesn't he ever see anything?
He didn't.
So that's why I'm saying I wasn't very-- maybe I was always analytically inclined, and didn't kid myself.
Remember that older people always-- like in the army, generals always fight the last war, not the next one.
The same thing with people.
They always view the times with the experience of the last war.
And remember, World War I, the Germans were the nice guys, and the Russians were the bad guys.
And you couldn't get it out of their blood.
And while, to me, it was quite clear, I was not burdened with the experience.
Experience can be a lot of burden.
No.

The only time I remember that one of our relatives was an alderman of the city, and quite prominent, decided to leave to go to Palestine-- and actually moved in '36-- showed up in '37 for a visit and told my mother, give me your son if you don't want to come.
That was the only time I remember.
[WHISPERS] Well, forget it.
That was a rhetorical question.
[LAUGHS] But-- but anyway, so that is the only thing I remember.
I think the-- the process of-- of the Nazi occupation and all the things that preceded it is a process of-- of creeping.
Everything in life is creeping.
You don't-- nothing happens, unless you have some historical event, like a catastrophic volcano disruption or something.
Everything in human life, including the stock market, is a gradual process.
And people grow into it, and adapt to it, and behave to the-- as they-- as the things evolve, responding to the details.
Very few people in life take a back look and take a perspective look at it.
Of course, they do suffer from Sunday football-- they are not Monday morning, you know, quarterback.
So, you know, you cannot really do that, frankly.
I mean, I cannot be too hard on them and say, well, how come they didn't see it?
Ask it today.
But I asked that question that time.
I was furious at some of my fathers and my uncles, whoever, pontificated next to me about the things that are going on and how they viewed it.
And I thought that they were headed all wrong.
And unfortunately, I was borne out.
I came back from the summer camp on 24th.
The mobilization was in full force.
August.
I'm sorry.
I thought it was clear.
Yeah, 24th of August, I came back from the-- from the camp.
And my father didn't want to do with me because my mother was at her stepmother's in Grudnow.
And he shipped me off to my aunt in the country.
They had a cottage, always.
So I stayed there the week.
And I came back on Thursday night, which was-- I don't know if you know what Thursday night was, it was 31st of August.
And I came home.
And my father picked me up at the railroad.
And I came home.
And I remember, I was with my friend in the morning, my best friend.
He used to come to my home.
And he came.
And we were wondering what happened to my mother.
And in the morning, we heard the pronunciation by the president of Poland that Poland was attacked.
And the Germans are crossing the border.
And immediately, there were already planes in the air.
And there were bombings.
And my mother was in a train that was bombed.
And she made it.
And she came in the afternoon.
So she was back.
And so the 1st of September, there were about three, four days-- the German population was clearly mostly pro-Nazi.
And obviously, things are going on.
Our own neighbors were Germans.
Oh, of course, first the Poles were attacking them and complaining that they may be spying.
Maybe they did.
They probably did.
They were sabotaged, whatever.
It didn't take too much.
I mean, take a few days and this-- the troops were in, I think, the 4th or the 5th.
Most Poland was taken over in four or five days.
The only city that defended itself for 28 days was Warsaw and the Citadel in Danzig, or Dansk, which was a military thing.
So the only city that defended itself for any length of time was Warsaw.
And the other cities were just taken over, tactically, immediately.
And so Lodz was.
On the 9th of September, I was back in school.
Schools were open.
So-- but immediately, the persecution started.
Well, the first one was the same one as for the Polish population-- all intellectuals, editors, newspapers.
Physicians, not so much, because I guess they were concerned about the health.
But lawyers or anybody in any prominent position, in politics, Jewish-- well certainly, Jewish for sure-- but Polish, as well-- they were taken out and just shot.
And so that was going on all the time.
And you can hear it, things happening.
And then, of course, the accent on the Jews, obviously, the persecution of those.
So we started the school.
And about not maybe two weeks over towards the end of September, we were already being asked to wear some bands or, as you know, special identifications.
You couldn't walk on the main street, the center street, you could only cross it.
And people were evacuated from their homes.
And again, mostly the better-offs.
And they were confiscating.
My school was immediately-- my school was in a very brand new building.
It was taken over immediately.
And we were sent to the girl's school.
So I went with the girls.
It became co-educational on the 9th of September.
And I think that something that maybe give you a feeling of what it looked like-- the-- the principal was still the same principal.
I think the original principal of the-- of the-- of my school was gone.
I met him, actually, in Israel later on.
But he-- this principal of the girl's school was still there.
Only very briefly, maybe a week or two.
But I remember that we had-- it was a class in English.
And I saw the picture of Mrs.
Pines right here in this book, which was very strange.
And she was standing there in class reading from the English book.
A door opened.
The principal walked in with two Gestapo officers.
And he turned to the-- and my teacher looked through the side of her eyes and saw who they were, and continued to speak in perfect German, because she was holding a German class.
And he turned to them and says, class in German.
They says, OK.
And they walked out.
And immediately, after they walked out, he came back and he says, that's it.
No more English books.
Nothing.
Everything back.
We are in Germany.
So I don't know why they were not alert to that.
This shows you what I think about grown-ups.
You know?
How come a principal of a school can think that, the Nazi occupation of Poland, he's going to run an English class?
That shows you the perception of the grown-ups.
So how did your life particularly change after the occupation?
Well, it became more and more restrictive, in the sense schools were moved from place to place.
About a month later, they closed that school.
And people were running away.
Many were disappearing.
There were about four or five-- well, maybe more-- maybe 10 Jewish high schools, including girl's-- a lot of girl's high schools.
I just realized that, looking at the book.
As the names came back.
And they were combined together.
So first, we were combined in another building with another couple of high schools.
Then again, and again.

And that process was going on till-- I don't remember the dates, but I would say, since we were herded into the ghetto about the end of February and the beginning of March, I would say that, over that period of time between, say, end of September towards January, we were probably moved at least three, four times.
And always shrinking.
And teachers, as well, disappearing.
And what did you think happened to these people?
Different things, probably.
Some of them are running away.
Some may have been-- you know, it's-- you live-- when you live in that environment-- remember, everything comes in steps, so you don't see the picture.
Whenever-- I don't try to be-- I'm not trying to be wise guy, but it's very difficult.
I'm trying now to sort of relay what we were at the time.
And if I'm going to start introducing hindsight, it's not going to be worth this process.
So I think that, if I try to go back to that, then I don't think that-- if you pardon me, I'm not trying to be rude-- but the question is kind of irrelevant.
You see what I'm trying-- you understand?
I'm making this point not to be rude, but to simply tell you that a question that this would be irrelevant, because you don't have a perspective.
You know?
Things happen one at a time.
You move into a new building.
Well, you adapt to a new building.
You adapt to new teachers.
Your adapt to new friends.
And you hear the problems that are going on.
Where did the people go?
You ask me the question, what happened to them?
I don't know.
One teacher disappeared.
Well, I heard that Mr.
Braverman probably took off to the Russian side.
How did he do that?
Well, obviously, it's not going to be publicized.
The other person would say, but they caught him, and, you know-- they-- he's an intellectual.
Maybe they knew it.
Maybe they arrested him.
What happened to him?
I know what happened to him today.
I didn't know what happened to him then.
Maybe they sent him to Warsaw.
Obviously, they didn't They took him probably in the field outside and shot him.
But so it's a-- this process of what is happening to you and, I think, change.
And remember, the Germans were very good at it, as we read now about how they ran concentration camps, how they told people that they'd take showers, and they gassed them.
So they were always very, apparently-- now it's hindsight-- aware of the fact that you don't do anything-- you know, you don't announce, we are going to burn and gas all the Jews tomorrow in Lodz.
Well, I presume there would be a riot.
Maybe not.
It wouldn't make any difference.
They'll gas them anyway.
But the point is, they didn't do that.
When they tried to do it in Warsaw, we had a Warsaw uprising, whatever it is.
What I am saying is that-- maybe not.
Maybe I drew some wrong conclusions, too.
I take it back, about the Warsaw ghetto-- there were other circumstances.
But what I'm saying is that, when you are in it, you are like a worm in cheese to say the best.
Other than the external circumstances, you still went to school.
You still had friends.
You still tried to get a good grade.
You still didn't like to do homework.
You still met a nice girlfriend, maybe, or whatever it was.
I don't know.
It was-- life is wonderful in the sense that it's very, very-- insist on normalcy.
That's our survival, really.
If I can define a survival, it's the desire for normalcy.
And that is always going to be the paramount drive in human behavior.
And I presume that-- so to ask this question, if a friend defended me, would be the same kind of a hurt?
If somebody looked at me, if a teacher was not nice to me, or if you-- you know, I would say that I can't think of anything-- what does a 14-year-old kid do anyway?
Do the same thing.
So when were you told you would have to move into the ghetto?
They started moving in about February.
End of February they decided to--
It was a terrible winter.
Oh, that was one of the worst winters.
Very cold.
And I remember my mother trying to get some coal to put in the furnace.
Anyway, we were told that everybody has to move out in the street.
Those streets are going to be closed.
And those streets, nobody can live there and nobody can live here.
And slowly, we have to more into the old city.
It was a very bad part of the city, needless to say.
And a lot of Jews lived there.
Very poor.
Lodz was a very big industrial place and had a very big proletariat.
And was a lot of people were unemployed, perennially.
Right?
There was a lot of poverty.
You know, there is this concept among Gentiles, I presume it goes around, about Jews being rich.
Well, I would suggest they go to Lodz in World War II and take a look.
And it was poor.
By the way, maybe I am partially guilty of the same opinion.
I thought Jews were always well-off, until I showed up there and I realized that the majority were actually starving poor.
And that's why they shoved us into it, obviously.
There were some-- of course, there were Poles living there, Gentiles.
And my father's best customer-- my father went from bad to worse financially and business-wise.
So he wasn't selling any more to builders.
He was selling to carpenters.
And one of his carpenters, a friend, was a German, who was married to a Pole.
His wife was Polish, not German, because at that time, they intermarried, obviously.
They lived in this city for 200 years.
And they had one son, who got my tennis racket.
And anyway, he was very friendly to my father.
And he lived in that area.
He wasn't that rich, obviously.
And of course, he made some deal with Jews who wanted to go there.
And he moved to their apartment.
He didn't do it necessarily because he wanted to throw them out.
Actually, he used to bring food to us.
And the interesting thing was, this father, his life was Polish, and his son was raised as a Pole.
But as soon as the Germans came in, they all became the so-called, Volksdeutsche, which is the Germans of the occupied territories.
And strangely enough, he was warning us always about his son, because he's going to join Hitler, he's going to be, you know, a German.
And of course, the father couldn't stand that.
And so he bring us food.
And he arranged for us to move into one of his-- he had a building there.
A building-- it was four rooms, and an outhouse, and a little closet in front of it.
And so we moved there.
And he would come and see us.
And he helped us to move there.
So we did not suffer from the terrible situation of moving to a ghetto, not knowing where to go.
They would herd three, four families in one room.
You know, people would just go in and try to occupy places.
And it was a mess.
So at least we had a very orderly transfer, because Mr.
[NON-ENGLISH], which was his name, and was a very fine man-- helped us to move there.
And at least, even though we got in the room, which was probably smaller-- just about my office size-- it was a little stove with the chimney is going up.
Actually, I cut myself on the chimney once.
And here it is.
So anyway-- and so that's why we-- so we had a transition which was less frantic.
And were you the only family then living in these quarters?
In that room.
One room.
There were four rooms.
Right.
One family got the largest room with the kitchen.
And that was the family they gave him an apartment, which was a very nice apartment in the city.
They exchanged.
And did you all share kitchen and sanitary facilities?
No, no, no.
Sanitary-- outhouse.
And so the only thing that a few-- now, we had a little front alcove, kind of.
Two doors, wooden door, and then you walked into the room.
And in that place, we were probably-- once the outhouse became uninhabitable-- because take the doors and everything-- we would use the pots there, and then take it out.
And a kitchen?
No kitchen.
One room.
One room.
There was a stove, a tiny stove with a chimney going up-- with pipe going up.
Well, you couldn't bring very much.
Well, we already had very little because, remember, we lived in a very small place, because we were already pretty well down on the ropes, as far as the financial situation was concerned, my father.
So we didn't come from any palaces, so we had to move several rooms.
We had a room, maybe two-- one room and furniture and things.
We moved very little.
We couldn't take any cabinets, for example.
There was no space for that.
And you took your briefcase?
And I took my briefcase, yes.
I carried it all the time during the ghetto.
